document.source_title: A Tale of Two Speeches: U.S.-Russian Relations Through the Lens of Munich
document.summary: This podcast episode focuses on U.S.-Russian relations, tracing the evolution from Vladimir Putin's 2007 speech at the Munich Security Conference to Russian Prime Minister Medvedev's speech in 2016. The discussion highlights interviews with various experts and politicians, illustrating the fluctuating nature of these relations over nearly a decade. The episode aims to provide insights into the complexities of international diplomacy and security issues surrounding Russia.
key_takeaways: The podcast discusses U.S.-Russian relations from 2007 to 2016.
It features speeches from Vladimir Putin and Dmitry Medvedev at the Munich Security Conference.
Interviews include insights from Richard Fontaine, Senator John McCain, and others.
The narrative shows a cycle of relations going from bad to good and back to bad.
The episode is part of a new podcast format focusing on international relations.
It is sponsored by American University’s School of International Service.
document.why_it_matters: Understanding the evolution of U.S.-Russian relations is crucial for comprehending current geopolitical dynamics and the implications for global security. This podcast provides a narrative that connects historical speeches to present-day issues, offering valuable insights for policymakers and the public.

claims.claim_text: U.S.-Russian relations have fluctuated significantly from 2007 to 2016.
claims.evidence_text: Between 2007 and 2016, U.S.-Russian relations have gone from bad to good (sort of) to bad again.
